item.title: Virtue and a Sledgehammer Preview – Therapy is for people without sledgehammers
item.content:encoded:
If you habitually put on a podcast for your commute, for walking the dog or doing the dishes, chances are you’ve been told that you need personal therapy in the few seconds before you manage and mash the skip ahead button 8 times. Therapy and counselling can be good for your soul, it’s true, but it can also take time and work to find your way through the troubles you’ve faced in your life. Virtue and a Sledgehammer proposes a different kind of therapeutic release. Can you guess what it is?

The body of Pratelle’s mother, Merche, is in the trunk of her car, and she so damn angry about this that she want to break stuff. How about her hometown? How about taking a sledgehammer to her past? Venting her emotions at how much has changed by tearing it all down?

That seems to be the plan, but she’s crashed it in the process of bringing Merche back home – running over two humanoid robots in the process. For the rest of the way, Pratelle needs to go on foot if she’s going to find her sister. Good thing she’s got that sledgehammer with her.

As a game, Virtue and a Sledgehammer is barbarically simple. You stomp purposefully through the world and wield a sledgehammer to smash through things that are in your way. Initially it’s your standard brick buildings and old walls, but then you come to a house with an android stood out front, contemplating life, and talking about who they are. Stand there for a few moments and they’ll talk about their own mother, their temper, how often they masturbate and eventually confess a desire for their life to be ended.

I stood there for a while, letting them cycle through missives. Is this a person? Or, just a loosely programmed automaton. In the end I lifted my sledgehammer and slammed them down into the ground. The second robot that I found, though, gave me more pause. The Warden addressed Pratelle by name in a cutscene, suggested that she see a doctor after the car crash. Questions if you crashed on purpose. Pratelle’s thoughts are simply to smash them out of the way, refusing to take counsel from a glorified microwave, as she puts it.

“We’ve always found a lot of these connections between the intentions of the character and the empathy that people project onto the robots,” Creative Director Jordi de Paco told us. “They are just robots and the character explicitly keeps saying that these are just tin cans, talking microwaves, toasters, but even with all the statements from the character, when people play they are like ‘Oooh, I don’t want to hit this!’ and I don’t want it to be like that. I mean, I hate that because it happens, but I understand it’s a characteristic of the human experience, and we later on in the game try to exploit that.

“We understand you are feeling compassion, but you shouldn’t.”

I continue to have mixed feelings as I carry on through the demo. All of these robots look the same, with boxy white bodies and limbs connected with black tubes for joints, and then with a 3:4 text display on their head, but they all act differently. Some will cower and run away, others will immediately come at you and fight, there are those trying to backup data in one building, there’s one that claims ownership over a small parcel of land. There’s non-zero chance that these are people, and Pratelle cannot see that anymore. She can only blame them.

She also can’t use doors. The only way to get through the world is to smash everything, whether it’s a wall, a window, a generator powering a locked gate. Jordi said “We considered it, but we actually like how instinctively it marks and it informs the level design. People are drawn towards doors, but they cannot interact with them. The only way is to smash them because the game is about that. It’s also a statement – this character would never go ‘Hello? Is anyone there?'”

Virtue and a Sledgehammer absolutely leans into representing the more destructive ways that people try to cope with their traumas, and it comes out the other side allowing you to indulge the typical behaviours of gamers, pushing the boundaries of what you can do, searching nooks and crannies for secrets and so on. That’s something new for Deconstructeam.

Jordi said, “Deconstructeam has in the past released pretty narrative, text-heavy games like The Cosmic Wheel Sisterhood or The Red Strings Club, and for this one, it really helped that this last year I was hired to help on some other projects – I wrote Ninja Gaiden Ragebound, and I wrote for Denshattack! – [….] and the director of those games always kept telling me that I need to communicate all of this stuff, and I only have, like, five lines to do it. So I brought that experience back to Virtue. […]

“Before that, we didn’t want to resort to video game gimmicks, like collecting stuff, but now I understand why developers do it – it’s really hard to populate a whole game only with fully-fledged narrative stuff. But at the same time, it’s not a matter of if you can do it, because it just kept destroying the pace of the game to just get questions and stuff like that. We want to reward people for exploration, but we don’t want to stop them with text all the time.”

As you explore, there’s various points that Virtue starts to reveal the roots of what is driving Pratelle on. Glassy shapes can be found and smashed, the white ones immediately dropping you into a memory, while the red ones flash an image on screen and adding a pip to a counter, eventually dropping you into a strange memory palace with a giant vision of Pratelle’s sister watching over, leading into another story moment.

These memories play out and give you options for how to act, allowing you to self insert into Pratelle’s character. For example, Nina asks Pratelle what she wants to be when she grows up, and the instinct is naturally to choose between a handful of jobs. But that doesn’t satisfy Nina. What does Pratelle want to *be?* Happy, maybe? But even that is merely a state of being, and she’s pushed for further introspection.

In another moment, Pratelle finds Merche stood out in the woods, naked and begging for forgiveness. It feels surreal, like a dream for Pratelle, but it’s actually finding her mother sleepwalking at best or losing her grasp on reality and her person at worst. Do you listen in and find out what she means, as she talks about her children? Or do you interrupt and bring her back home. It’s a more simple compassionate decision to make, but at this early point in the game, we’re still trying to figure out what has led Pratelle down her most destructive path.

There’s a pleasing balance between these sides of Virtue and a Sledgehammer. Yes, there’s smashing things and delighting in the way that building crumble, but you’re drawn into the various ways that the robots react to you, potentially listening in on their conversations before you smash them to bits, and the shifts to memories and choosing your input into a fractured relationship add depth and meaning to the experience.

“It was an iterative process,” Jordi said about finding this balance between destruction and story, “and it wasn’t like this until maybe three or four months ago. It has been two years in development and it was only recently that we cracked the code. We were super worried for what we were going to do to save the pacing of the game, and suddenly tried this idea and it’s like, OK, this is it. Finally, we managed to make it work.”

You can play Virtue and a Sledgehammer now with a demo available on Steam ahead of a planned release later this year.



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/06/15/virtue-and-a-sledgehammer-preview-therapy-is-for-people-without-sledgehammers/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Erosion Is an Open-World Roguelike With a Fresh Take on Meta-Progression
item.content:encoded:
When I played Erosion, I couldn't help but draw immediate comparisons to Enter the Gungeon. The initial gameplay feels pretty similar, and that's not a bad thing—I love Gungeon. The more I played it though, the more I learned how much Erosion really stands on its own.

For one, there's an entire open world here. Sure, your kidnapped daughter awaits you in the Pillar, the game's main dungeon, but you could also go literally anywhere else. Hop on your hoverbike, explore, and find villages, towns, farms, and whatever else.

The catch is: every third time you die, the timeline moves forward by 10 years. This changes a lot in the world, including enemies, towns, and of course, your daughter's age. The ramifications of this aren't clear yet, but the implications are intriguing.

Diving Into the Dungeon

The gameplay feels like a slick twin-stick shooter, and the real fun kicks in once you gather passive power-ups that work toward a build. I started off with a shotgun, which was devastating up close as it spread five bullets. Then I found a power-up that added an extra bullet to the spread. Another made the bullets bounce between enemies, and another extended the bounce's effects even further.

There were other power-ups I simply skipped because I felt like I had a strong build already going. I was clearing rooms, weaving through enemy bullets, and laying down the law like a cowboy should. There were even times when I'd pull out my sidearm, a whip, instead, both to conserve ammo and farm for some more. The whip has a higher chance of dropping ammo, which is a clever survival mechanic.

What really tied the build together was the one active ability I found, which created a clone of my character. That clone would mirror my movements and actions, effectively doubling my shotgun output for a couple of seconds. The downside though, is that the clone could be damaged, effectively doubling my risk of getting hurt. Some risks are worth the reward, so I timed my use of it as best I could.

I cruised through these first few floors. The input feedback was quick, apparent, and satisfying. I always felt in control of my movement, and any time I took a hit, I usually knew it was my fault for not dodging. Sometimes it was hard to tell exactly where the hitbox is and where your invincibility frames are in the dodge roll, though that could just be a byproduct of the game still being in development.

Unfortunately, I died to the boss thanks to its complex bullet pattern. When I respawned on the surface, the developer directed me to instead drive north, away from the entrance to the dungeon. There, I found a farm full of bandits and cleared them out. By exploring more there, I could find permanent powerups, like one that would increase how many passives I could carry in the dungeon.

It's a nice twist on the roguelike formula where permanent meta-progression can be found outside of the "core" gameplay loop. It sounds exciting in the long term, as it'll help keep the gameplay fresh. That abandoned farm could become a thriving community in the future—who's to say?

I imagine the timeline mechanic will also hold more surprises as you spend more time with Erosion. I should note that when I died earlier, I still had two more attempts in this timeline before the world would jump forward by 10 years. So I didn't experience any of the time mechanics. Nonetheless, it holds a lot of intrigue for me. My guess is that more upgrades will become available in the overworld as time continues to progress, though then you run the risk of your character's daughter aging more. Whether that's a bad thing is to be determined.

Erosion has no release window yet, but you can wishlist it on Steam and even request access to the playtest.

Erosion was previewed on a PC at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/erosion-is-open-world-roguelike-with-fresh-take-on-meta-progression
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Great Indie Games We Saw at Summer Game Fest
item.content:encoded:
If you look at our Summer Game Fest coverage this year, our team saw well over 60 games and went hands-on with the vast majority, ranging from small indie titles to the biggest AAA games coming in the future. Here's some great indie games from a variety of genres we saw at the show.

Bub's Unique Art and Tone Will Stick With You

Developer: Paperfrog | Publisher: Paperfrog

Bub was one of the most interesting games I've seen in a while. Immediately, the style will grab you. Everything on-screen is handmade in some way. That could be cut out construction paper, figures made of dry erase marker on clear sheets, or just about anything you can think of.

What you see on screen changes dramatically from scene to scene, not sticking to one visual medium entirely, to fit the tone of that particular scene. While all are different, there is a cohesion in the style that threads through it all.

The main style is that hand-drawn marker on paper that makes the world feel messy, confused, and somewhat off. It fits the theme of the game as you take on the role of an artist that's sick who is trying to make art of his past memories while he's still able.

Another interesting aspect is the stream of consciousness sort of mumblings of Bub as you walk around. Whether he's making an odd comment on something or you just hear his thoughts when talking to someone, it settles you into this wonderfully weird world.

Bub releases in 2027 on PC.

D-topia is a Charming Dystopian Puzzler

Developer: Marumittu Games | Publisher: Annapurna Interactive

D-topia has a wonderful style that sucks you in with its charm, lightheartedness, and the general upbeat nature of everything going on. An AI has everything sorted to you, all your basic necessities provided to you. What could go wrong?

The backdrop is this dystopian future where you learn more about society and the residents of D-topia. Of course, not is all as it seems, so part of the game is unraveling the mystery of what's going on.

The core gameplay, however, are logic puzzles that are visually simple but so satisfying when everything comes together.

The puzzles I had access to were about moving a set of numbered blocks to the correct spot. There were lines to cross that could increase or decrease the number, sometimes having numbers add up to specific numbers when placed next to another numbered block was important, mechanics where you had to park a square to unblock an area to move a different square, and a whole lot more.

I only saw one type of puzzle, and I know there is more the further you get into the game. If you're a puzzle fan, D-topia is one to keep an eye on.

D-topia releases July 14th on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, Nintendo Switch, Nintendo Switch 2.

Demi and the Fractured Dream Feels Like a Classic Zelda Game

Developer: Yarn Owl Studios | Publisher: Annapurna Interactive

My brief time with Demi and the Fractured Dream was reminiscent of a classic Zelda game like Ocarina of Time or Windwaker in just a few minutes of playing it.

Unfortunately, my time with Demi wasn't long, but I did get to experience a small snapshot of a dungeon. Zelda vibes jumped out right away with locking on an enemy, though there's a little more action with Demi with its more hack and slash style combat and combos.

One big difference from something like Zelda is the ability to jump. In the boss fight in particular, jumping to miss shockwaves or even the swipe was key to winning.

Environmental puzzles played out simply, such as using a leaf I had to blow wind and uncover something in sand, knocking an object flying through the air towards a target, and even using the leaf on a platform as a way to propel myself around.

Everything about Demi and the Fractured Dream felt like a classic adventure in a wonderfully nostalgic way.

Demi and the Fractured Dream releases February 2027 on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, Ninteno Switch, and Nintendo Switch 2.

Hoa 2's Hand-Painted Aesthetic Is Gorgeous

Developer: Skrollcat Studio | Publisher: PM Studios

Cute, cozy games are in, and Hoa 2 is looking to cash in on that trend with its lightweight puzzle gameplay and hand-painted art style. You control a little fairy named Hoa (prounounced "hwa") who's trying to revive a robot. Another robot guides the fairy, explaining how to gather Fuel to bring the robot back to life.

Hoa explores the landscape, which feels mostly linear—it's hard to get lost, though sometimes you can skip to the next objective and then have to backtrack. You'll run into cute creatures, like giant crabs or rolypolys, all of which help you platform around the world.

Your main objective lies inside of mini dungeons, where you have to play a game inspired by pipe dream. You press a button to start a stream of Fuel, and is it moves along the pipes, you have to run ahead and rotate joints to get the Fuel to the end. It's simple, if not a little too simple. But for an easygoing game to kick back with and enjoy beautiful visuals, Hoa 2 is a bright and vibrant pick.

Hoa 2 will release in 2026 on PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and Nintendo Switch 2.

Shot One Fighters is a Great Singleplayer Roguelike Fighting Game

Developer: Red Moon Workshop | Publisher: Red Moon Workshop

Shot One Fighters is such an obviously smart idea I can't believe it's the first time I've seen a game like this. One thing fighting games usually lack is a compelling or long-lasting singleplayer component. Shot One Fighters is the answer.

There are a lot of standard roguelike elements, with a Slay the Spire sort of map to move through for a run. There are even rest points and a shop to buy things at.

You start with a base fighter and one of the rewards after each match may be a new move or a modification to something you have. Think buffs like projectiles do more damage or you can get a new move like a roll.

Each run you build a unique fighter whose combos and playstyle change each time, which is a refreshing way to engage with fighting game mechanics. So much of a fighting game is memorization and muscle memory with certain characters, where Shot One Fighters has an element of that but is more about engaging directly with fighting game mechanics. I'd imagine the more you play, the more well-rounded a player you become.

In any case, I think fighting game fans should keep a close eye on this one.

Shot One Fighters is coming to PC and possibly more in the future. They will be launching a Kickstarter soon, too.

Sonic Pico Park Will Make & Break Friendships

Developer: TECOPARK | Publisher: PICO PARK Inc

Pico Park is a cute co-op 2D puzzle game where each player controls a little guy, and all these little guys have to work together to complete each level. Sonic Pico Park twists the formula a bit by adding four Sonic characters to the mix: Sonic, Tails, Knuckles, and Amy. All four of them can spin dash, which gives a huge burst of speed and can push other characters a bit. On top of that, Tails can fly and carry others under him (though three will weigh him down), and Knuckles can glide across gaps.

Sonic Pico Park is a lighthearted, family-friendly good time. Cooperation is key here; it's impossible to do any puzzle alone, and anyone who tries to be a hero will end up ruining the puzzle for everyone. So when you want to have a relaxed time with some friends (or make some new enemies in the process), keep an eye out for this one.

Sonic Pico Park has no release date, and it will come out on Steam with other platforms to be determined.

Super Yooka-Laylee Kart is a Fun Retro Kart Game

Developer: Playtonic Games | Publisher: Playtonic Friends

Super Yooka-Laylee Kart is a modern refresh of the original Super Mario Kart, which works out great. Largely it still feels like an old school kart game, but there are a lot of modern upgrades.

The first thing is the art style. It is undeniably inspired by something like the original Super Mario Kart, but it has seen an upgrade in a similar way to the HD2D remakes of some classic RPGs. The models and pixel art is there, but so too are reflections on the water, beautiful lighting effects, and more.

As for the racing, it still is just as fast and squirrelly as those old kart games but has more modern elements like drift boosting as well. It's been in development less than a year, so I wouldn't be surprised to see more changed up in the future, too.

One of the biggest features, Super Yooka-Laylee Kart lets you customize races with a ton of options. Those range from adding collision between racers, making racers invisible, making racers small, changing boost power of drifts, making boosts on the tracks slow you down, mirror mode… Let's say the list is long.

Oh, it will also have 8 player local split screen racing. Chaos for sure.

Super Yooka-Laylee Kart has no release date or information on platforms yet.

Valor Mortis Blends Napoleon With Bloodborne

Developer: One More Level | Publisher: One More Level, Lyrical Games

I first saw Valor Mortis at PAX West last year and was absolutely blown away by its atmosphere and combat system. From the developers of Ghostrunner, it blends soulslike elements with weighty first-person combat, and its novel setting in an alternative version of the Napoleonic era hooked me. My demo at Summer Game Fest included a section titled "Chapter 7," and it definitely felt harder compared to the one from PAX.

I only had time to explore the level and couldn't make it to the boss. I ran into a good variety of enemies—one was a short little creature, and another was gigantic and would swing a huge two-handed weapon around. Between them were some average-sized zombified soldiers, some with swords, others with guns.

The combat is intense and visceral; you're up close with these dangerous enemies, having to block, parry, and dodge around them. It's easy to get overwhelmed if you aren't careful. The new weapons I tried were exciting though. The rapier pierces precisely, meaning it can burst weak points for maximum damage (something that previously only guns could do). And the musket felt pretty satisfying to use from afar, doing lethal damage at the cost of a long reload time.

Valor Mortis will release on October 13 (after a fresh delay from the September apocalypse) on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S.



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/great-indie-games-we-saw-at-summer-game-fest

item.title: Google Earth’s flight simulator is now available in your browser
item.content:encoded:
Andrew Liszewski

is a senior reporter who’s been covering and reviewing the latest gadgets and tech since 2006, but has loved all things electronic since he was a kid.

On Friday Google announced it was making a relatively unknown feature of Google Earth available to a wider audience. The desktop version of Google Earth has had a hidden flight simulator mode since 2007, but it’s now easily accessible through a web browser without having to download or install any apps.

To take to the virtual skies you’ll need to open the Google Earth website and then click on the “Explore Earth” button found near the top right corner of the page. From there you’ll find the “Flight Simulator” mode accessible as the last option in the Tool’s section of the site’s top menu bar. For the best experience you’ll first want to find an interesting part of the world to explore as the first locale that loaded for me was in the middle of a featureless ocean. You’ll also want to make sure you change Google Earth’s basemap type from Map to Satellite for a photorealistic experience.

Google has shared a help page detailing how to use your keyboard or mouse to control your aircraft. While the experience isn’t as complex or realistic as you’ll get in games like Microsoft Flight Simulator, the Google Earth simulator will still require a bit of practice to point your aircraft where you want it to go without crashing. But should your flight end with an unplanned impact with the terrain the simulator will pause and give you the option to reset your aircraft to a safe altitude again.

Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates.

  • Andrew Liszewski


https://www.theverge.com/tech/949749/google-earth-flight-simulator-browser-experimental

item.title: The Head Of Xbox Game Studios Resigns Ahead Of Mass Layoffs
item.content:encoded:
Craig Duncan is stepping down as head of Xbox Game Studios, according to a new report by The Game Business. He held the role for less than two years and previously led Sea of Thieves maker Rare for over a decade. The move comes as teams inside Xbox brace for the latest round of mass layoffs and potential studio closures following new CEO Asha Sharma’s announcement of a “reset” last week.

Duncan was in charge of overseeing a sprawling network of game studios which included everything from Halo Studios and The Coalition to Double Fine and Undead Labs. The industry veteran was responsible not only for helping get major blockbusters like the upcoming Fable shipped, but also navigating the company’s evolving strategy around exclusives and multiplatform releases. Xbox Game Studios will now report up to chief content officer Matt Booty.

According to The Game Business, Duncan wrote in an email to staff: “When I stepped into the role of leading XGS 20 months ago, my purpose was to serve our studios, our teams, and the people making our games. Together, we set out to deliver high-quality games, strengthen the cultural fabric across our studios, and help shape the future of the business. I’m proud to say we delivered many flawless launches that drove business success for the company.”

Xbox has recently signaled plans to invest more resources in its biggest blockbusters while winding down the lest profitable parts of its portfolio. “We are the fortunate stewards of industry-defining franchises that have enormous potential and player demand, but we have not adequately funded them to compete and win,” Sharma told staff last week. “At the same time, as we saw this past weekend at Showcase, a reliable pipeline of first- and third-party exclusives and new IP are critical to our success. We need to reassess the balance between these and our investment priorities for the next 5 years.”

Xbox Game Studios chief of staff, Louise O’Connor, is reportedly also stepping down. She was previously an executive producer at Rare until its long-in-development fantasy game Everwild was canceled last year in the company’s last round of sweeping cuts.

Xbox did not respond to a request for comment.



https://kotaku.com/the-head-of-xbox-game-studios-resigns-ahead-of-mass-layoffs-2000706634

item.title: God of War Laufey ‘Could’ Release as Soon as Early 2027, It’s Claimed
item.content:encoded:
A new GOW game starring Faye from the reboot games wasn’t the biggest shock in the world when it was announced during the Sony State of Play Showcase for June 2026. But it would be a surprise if it were released in the next few months, which is apparently a possibility.

The rumored God of War spin-off game was revealed during the Sony State of Play Showcase in June 2026.

As expected, Faye is the main character, temporarily taking over our strong, bearded awarrior—as Santa Monica has said God of War isn’t done with Kratos—and all eyes are on the release date.

God of War Laufey Targeting First Half of 2027?

Now, I must reinforce that this is a claim, and one that ‘could change,’ which is expected as you never know anymore. Release dates are being shifted left, right, and center, even for the biggest games on the market.

However, reliable industry insider NateTheHate was asked on June 13 about God of War Laufey’s release date:

‘Last I heard the target was first half 2027. Could change but that was a hope at one point.’

It’s a bit of a double-edged sword, then, but it does support Jason Schrier’s claim that God of War Laufey will ‘Definitely’ release before 2028.

Rumors suggest Santa Monica has been developing Laufey for some time, so it’s more than reasonable to assume it’s far along in the development cycle.

Whether it releases in early 2027 is one thing, but it seems extremely likely that we’ll get GOW Laufey in 2027. This should keep us all ticking over until the massive God of War original trilogy remake that is also in the works.

If you missed it, check out the first gameplay footage of God of War Laufey.

Head to the Insider Gaming Discord server to discuss GOW Laufey, the upcoming remakes, or anything else gaming-related.

The IG stories don’t end here: First details on The Witcher multiplayer game have reportedly been revealed, a Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 Nintendo Switch 2 release window has been outed, and The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time Remake description has been secretly changed by Nintendo.

Written by

Evergreen Editor

Andy is Insider Gaming's Senior Evergreen Editor and has been in the games journalism sphere in one way or another for over 7 years. His video game taste is as…

item.title: Dead by Daylight unveils modding support, a visual overhaul, and a Scooby-Doo collaboration during 10th anniversary celebrations
item.content:encoded:
A full decade in, Behaviour Interactive's Dead by Daylight has settled into a comfortable rhythm of content updates and seasonal events, including some surprises. But as the asymmetrical horror game enters its 11th year, there are big changes coming, including mod support and a visual overhaul.

Those two headline additions were shared during Dead by Daylight's recent hours-long 10th anniversary broadcast, in which Behaviour confirmed official modding support - for the likes of user-made maps and modes - is coming in 2027. Additionally, the studio is implementing dynamic weather and visual refresh aiming to transform Dead by Daylight's ageing looks.

Behaviour also revealed the impending arrival of Friday the 13th's Jason (an impressive achievement considering the wobbly state of the franchise's rights over the years), a new original DLC chapter - The Life Road - coming later this month, and another famed slasher villain, Terrifier's Art the Clown, who's set to enter The Fog this November. Oh, and we can also expect a new map based on liminal spaces (which sounds like a perfect excuse for a Backrooms collab, perhaps), but it won't arrive before December.

A couple of cosmetics-only collaborations have also been confirmed, including a collection for metalcore band Ice Nine Kills and the return of Iron Maiden. We can also expect skins themed around Silent Hill, The Walking Dead, Diablo 4, and Scooby-Doo.

Less headline-grabbing but still notable, August sees the launch of Chorus of Sin, introducing new original (and celestial) killer The Judgment and new survivor Aurora. There's also an in-universe collaboration with Supermassive Games' Dead by Daylight spin-off The Casting of Frank Stone coming in early 2027 release. And amid all this, Behaviour is promising more events like The Black Banquet (running from mid-June to mid-July) and new seasonal rifts (challenge tracks of sorts with unique rewards) running throughout the year and into 2027.

And that's pretty much all the key stuff, aside from an update on that live-action Dead by Daylight movie adaptation.

Mentioned in this article

Dead by Daylight

Nintendo Switch, PC, PS4, PS5, Stadia, Xbox One, Xbox Series X/S

item.title: Blood of Dawnwalker Will Be ‘Similar to Classic Fallout Games’
item.content:encoded:
At first blush, that’s not a headline that makes much sense. After all, The Blood of Dawnwalker is an ambitious and brutal third-person vampire simulator with serious Witcher vibes, slated to be released this September. How could it possibly be similar to the classic run of Fallout games, a spate of isometric, turn-based post-apocalyptic RPG titles?

In a recent interview, the CEO of Rebel Wolves, the studio behind The Blood of Dawnwalker, one of this year’s most anticipated games, explained the shaping of this title, including post-launch plans.

The Blood of Dawnwalker Starts a Saga

In a recent interview with PAP Biznes, the CEO at Rebel Wolves, Konrad Tomaszkiewicz, sat down to talk about all things The Blood of Dawnwalker. This AAA third-person action title is set to drop on September 3rd, bringing about a game that’s steeped in mystery, innovation, and gruesome vampire-backed combat sequences.

If you’ve seen The Blood of Dawnwalker and think it looks like a Witcher game, you’re not far from the truth. Tomaszkiewicz worked at CDPR, most notably as the director on The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, released in 2015, and several of the studio’s employees came from that side of the industry.

In the interview, the Rebel Wolves CEO tried to describe the narrative concept in The Blood of Dawnwalker, relying on a series that comes out of left field for this comparison:

‘The Blood of Dawnwalker’ will be primarily a narrative sandbox, similar to the classic ‘Fallout’ games. Our goal is for the player to be able to decide which path they want to take to reach the end of the game.

(Thanks to PushSquare for the spot)

It’s nothing like Fallout in terms of the overarching gameplay elements, visual palette, or setting, but it’s like Fallout in that it has a branching narrative that can be driven by the player at all times.

The hope from Rebel Wolves is that The Blood of Dawnwalker, which has secured a spot on more than 1.5 million wishlists, does well enough at launch that it secures the studio’s financial future, allowing them to expand on the saga that’s waiting to be explored.

There could even be a spate of free DLCs released for The Blood of Dawnwalker after the game hits the market in September, all being well.

Everything is shaping up quite nicely for The Blood of Dawnwalker, and it stands firm as one of this year’s most promising games.

Are you looking forward to The Blood of Dawnwalker? Let us know your thoughts on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that ARC Raiders is getting a PvE map condition

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.
